blocks.text: The precious children of Zion, worth their weight in fine gold— how they are reckoned as earthen pots, the work of a potter's hands!
2 : , see 1.4n. were cheap and easily broken and discarded, while gold was an expensive and durable material. The children are likened to throwaway objects because no one can care for them.
Even the jackals offer the breast and nurse their young, but my people has become cruel, like the ostriches in the wilderness.
3 : , considered despicable scavengers. , thought to be cruel and neglectful parents (cf. Job 39.13–18). , not willingly but from desperation; they are no longer able to care for their children.
The tongue of the infant sticks to the roof of its mouth for thirst; the children beg for food, but no one gives them anything.
4 : , an expression meaning that no sound is uttered (Ezek 3.26; Ps 137.6; Job 29.10). The children are too weak even to cry.
Those who feasted on delicacies perish in the streets; those who were brought up in purple cling to ash heaps.
5 : , better “crimson” (cf. Isa 1.18).
For the chastisement of my people has been greater than the punishment of Sodom, which was overthrown in a moment, though no hand was laid on it.
6 : , whose punishment was legendary (Gen 19.24–25), was overthrown in an instant, whereas Jerusalem was made to suffer long agony.
Her princes were purer than snow, whiter than milk; their bodies were more ruddy than coral, their hairc like sapphire.
Now their visage is blacker than soot; they are not recognized in the streets. Their skin has shriveled on their bones; it has become as dry as wood.
7–8 : The bright colors associated with vigor have turned to black; now people are dried up.
Happier were those pierced by the sword than those pierced by hunger, whose life drains away, deprived of the produce of the field.
The hands of compassionate women have boiled their own children; they became their food in the destruction of my people.
4.1–10: The suffering of the siege.
The Lord gave full vent to his wrath; he poured out his hot anger, and kindled a fire in Zion that consumed its foundations.
The kings of the earth did not believe, nor did any of the inhabitants of the world, that foe or enemy could enter the gates of Jerusalem.

12 : The Judean belief in the inviolability of Zion, that God would never permit his city to be destroyed (see Pss 46.5; 48.3–8), is here attributed even to foreigners, a creative use of the “passersby” trope (see 2.15n).
It was for the sins of her prophets and the iniquities of her priests, who shed the blood of the righteous in the midst of her.
Blindly they wandered through the streets, so defiled with blood that no one was able to touch their garments.
13–14 : , those most associated with purity and visions are described as the most blind and defiled.
“Away! Unclean!” people shouted at them; “Away! Away! Do not touch!” So they became fugitives and wanderers; it was said among the nations, “They shall stay here no longer.”
15 : The public warning that lepers must give so that no one will come in contact with them (Lev 13.45–46).
The Lord himself has scattered them, he will regard them no more; no honor was shown to the priests, no favor to the elders.
4.11–16: God's wrathful punishment.
Our eyes failed, ever watching vainly for help; we were watching eagerly for a nation that could not save.
17 : , most likely Egypt, the major power opposed to Babylonia (see Jer 37.5–10; Isa 30.7). Contrary to what prophets like Jeremiah advised, Judah relied on help from another nation, not from God.
They dogged our steps so that we could not walk in our streets; our end drew near; our days were numbered; for our end had come.
Our pursuers were swifter than the eagles in the heavens; they chased us on the mountains, they lay in wait for us in the wilderness.
19 : Perhaps an allusion to the aftermath of the fall of Jerusalem, when Zedekiah and his soldiers fled the city and were pursued and captured by the Babylonian army (2 Kings 25.4–5).
The Lord's anointed, the breath of our life, was taken in their pits— the one of whom we said, “Under his shadow we shall live among the nations.”
20 : , royal epithets (1 Sam 24.7; 2 Sam 1.14,16). The reference is likely to Zedekiah, the last king of Judah, whose defeat signals the failure of the Davidic dynasty to protect the people.
Rejoice and be glad, O daughter Edom, you that live in the land of Uz; but to you also the cup shall pass; you shall become drunk and strip yourself bare.

21 : , an ironic statement. , an epithet parallel to (see 1.6n.) , a region in southern Jordan or northwestern Arabia, often connected with Edom (Gen 36.28; Jer 25.20; Job 1.1; 1 Chr 1.42). , of God's wrath (see 3.15; Jer 25.15–29; 49.12; 51.7; Hab 2.15–16), which will make Edom drunk. , expose your nakedness and be shamed as Jerusalem was (1.8–10).
The punishment of your iniquity, O daughter Zion, is accomplished, he will keep you in exile no longer; but your iniquity, O daughter Edom, he will punish, he will uncover your sins.

4.1–22: The community under siege in Jerusalem. The physical and social effects of starvation are graphically described. The progression is realistic: first the children are affected and then the adults. Degradation and debasement is the main theme; those once well-off are now destitute, those once healthy are dying or dead. The “before” and the “after,” is achieved by the contrast in color, as the rich palette of the colors of wealth and health (gold, scarlet, white, red, sapphire in vv. 1,2,5,7,8) is drained away to blackness. Heat is another dimension, the unrelenting heat of the summer siege, the dry and blackened skin (v. 8), the parched mouths (v. 4), God's burning anger setting fire to Zion (v. 11). There is no shade, no protection provided by God or the king (v. 20). Like chs 1 and 2, this chapter contains a single alphabetic acrostic and opens with the word (see 1.1n).
4.17–22: The voice of the community. As in 3.40–47, the speaker is “we,” describing the final days of the siege and the fall of Jerusalem.

21–22 : A curse on Edom, a neighbor of Judah who was a vassal to Babylonia. Edom is often singled out for negative comment in postexilic literature (Ezek 35; Ob 11; Ps 137.7). Edom is synonymous with Esau (Gen 36.1) and therefore the traditional enemy or rival of Jacob/Israel (=Judah); cf. Mal 1.2–5.
22 : The most hopeful sentiment in the book (cf. Joel 3).
